summary: Spolsky introduces Activity Based Planning as a superior alternative to feature-driven software design. Rather than listing features and hanging them off menus, designers should identify the activities users actually perform and build the interface around those workflows. He illustrates this with a greeting card application and the pivotal discovery during Excel 5.0's design that most users were simply keeping lists, not building financial models. The post also advocates creating detailed imaginary user personas to ground design decisions in empathy rather than programmer assumptions. Together, these techniques form a six-step process for designing software that feels natural and guides users toward their goals.
key_points: Feature-driven design leads to programs that have all the right capabilities but don't flow — users can't discover how to accomplish their goals
Activity Based Planning reframes design around what users are trying to do, which naturally suggests features and priorities that a feature list wouldn't reveal
Observing real Excel users revealed that list-keeping was far more common than financial modeling, leading to major features like AutoFilter and multi-user editing
Lotus Improv failed because it optimized for complex multidimensional models while making the most common activity — keeping lists — nearly impossible
Creating detailed imaginary user personas prevents programmers from overestimating user sophistication and grounds design decisions in empathy
money_quotes: People sit down and they don't know what it does, and they don't know how to accomplish what they want.
The key insight is to figure out the activity that the user is doing, and focus on making it easy to accomplish that activity.
Instead of thinking about your program like a programmer, you're thinking about it like the user.
We only had to watch about five customers using the product before we realized that an enormous number of people just use Excel to keep lists.

Improv is now a footnote in history. Search for it on the web, and the only links you'll find are from very over-organized storeroom managers who have, for some reason, made a web site with an inventory of all the stuff they have collecting dust.
One of the best ways to decide which features get done, and which features get left out, is to evaluate which features support the most important user activities.
Thinking about a 'real' person gives you the empathy you need to make a feature that serves that person's need.
Good UI sells software, but it also makes people happy, because people are happy when they accomplish the task they wanted to accomplish.
key_insight: Great software design starts not with a list of features but with understanding the specific activities users are trying to accomplish, then building the interface to guide them through those activities naturally.
